metadata.title: Agnostic Learnability of Halfspaces via Logistic Loss

sections.title: Setting.
content: Binary classification:given by distribution P on Rd× {-1, +1}; we have i.i.d. samples from P.
Linear classifier:x ↦ ⟨x, w⟩.
Goal:compete with the optimal solution ū, where
Logistic regression (LR):let ℓlog(z) := ln(1 + e-z), and let
denote the population logistic risk of w over P.

sections.title: Contributions.
content: General P: 1 - OPT lower bound (Ben-David et al., 2012).
Isotropic log-concave distributions: Ω(OPT) lower bound (Diakonikolas et al., 2020).
"Well-behaved" sub-exponential distributions: O(√OPT) upper bound with SGD (Frei et al., 2021).
Standard concentration and anti-concentration conditions.
Example: a mixture of log-concave dist. (e.g., Gaussian).
Ω(√OPT) lower bound for well-behaved sub-exp dist.; matching O(√OPT) upper bound from (Frei et al., 2021).
O(OPT) upper bound with "radial Lipschitzness."
Diakonikolas et al. (2020) designed a nonconvex SGD that achieves O(OPT) + ε risk using O(d/ε4) samples.
Other prior algorithms achieving O(OPT) + ε risk involve solving multiple rounds of convex program.
We design a simple two-phase convex program that achieves O(OPT ln(1/OPT)) + ε risk using O(d/ε2) samples,withoutradial Lipschitzness.

sections.title: Lower bound.
content: We construct a distribution Q with three parts Q1, Q2, Q4.
1. Q1consists of two squares with edge length √OPT/2 and density 1; one has center (√2/2, -√2/2) and label -1, the other has center (-√2/2, √2/2) and label +1.
2. Q2is supported on [0, √OPT] × [0, 1] and [-√OPT, 0] × [-1, 0] with density 1, and the label is given by sign(x1).
3. Q4is the uniform distribution over the unit ball with density q4and label sign(x1).
To minimize Rlogon Q4, we should go to infinity along (1, 0).
Q1keeps w* bounded with ||w*|| = O(1/√OPT).
Q2drags w* above by an angle of Ω(√OPT).

figures.description: Geometric construction of distribution Q showing regions Q1, Q2 and the unit ball for the lower bound proof.

sections.title: Upper bound: radial Lipschitzness.
content: To exclude Q and overcome the lower bound, we make the following assumption for LR.
Radial Lipschitzness:There exists a measurable function κ : R+→ R+such that for any two-dimensional subspace V, letting pVdenote the density of the projection of feature distribution onto V, then
Holds if pVis Lipschitz, even non-log-concave ones (e.g., Gaussian mixtures).
Does not hold for general log-concave distributions.
Does not hold for Q, non-Lipschitz near the vertical axis.

sections.title: Upper bound: logistic regression.
content: Projected gradient descent (PGD):let w0:= 0, and
Proof ideas:under the given conditions, suppose ŵ satisfies Rlog(ŵ) ≤ Rlog(||w̄||ū) + ε', then
Cκ= O(ln(1/OPT)2) for Lipschitz density.
The global optimizer w* of Rlogsatisfies ||w*|| = Ω(1/√OPT); PGD also ensures wthas small ε' and ||wt|| = Ω(1/√OPT).

sections.title: Upper bound: two-phase convex program.
content: However, the global minimizer of Rhis 0; if we find ŵ by global minimization, as ε' gets small, ||ŵ|| also gets small.
LR finds unit v that is Õ(√OPT) away from ū.
Then minimize Rhover D := {w | ⟨w, v⟩ ≥ 1}.∀w ∈ D, ||w|| ≥ 1.||w̄||ū may not in D, but (1 + Õ(OPT))||w̄||ū ∈ D!
||w̄||ū may not in D, but (1 + Õ(OPT))||w̄||ū ∈ D!
Use SGD for better sample complexity.
